subsystem: qpdf ASCII85 stream-decode pipeline. Pl_ASCII85Decoder is a qpdf 'Pipeline' filter that decodes the ASCII base-85 (ASCII85 / '<~ ... ~>') encoding used in PDF stream objects. flush() takes the up-to-5 base-85 digit characters accumulated in the object's internal inbuf[], converts a group of 5 base-85 digits back into a 32-bit value, splits it into up to 4 output bytes, and forwards the decoded bytes to the next pipeline stage via getNext()->write(). It runs while parsing untrusted PDF content streams / stream data.
provenance: Identified as qpdf by strong naming conventions: the 'Pl_' class-name prefix is qpdf's convention for Pipeline subclasses, 'Pl_ASCII85Decoder' is a qpdf pipeline class, and QTC::TC("libtests", ...) is qpdf's QUtil 'test coverage' instrumentation macro (scope string "libtests"). The sample banner 'primevul_validation_0391_fixed' plus the CWE-787 filename tag indicate this is the post-fix revision of a PrimeVul out-of-bounds-write sample. No SPDX or copyright header is present in the excerpt; qpdf is authored by Jay Berkenbilt and licensed Apache-2.0.
trust_boundary: The bytes in this->inbuf[] originate from an attacker-controllable PDF file: a PDF stream's ASCII85-encoded data is fed byte-by-byte into Pl_ASCII85Decoder::write() (not shown), which validates/collects base-85 digit characters into inbuf[] and advances this->pos, calling flush() when a group completes or at end-of-stream. Therefore inbuf[] contents, and critically the value of this->pos, are functions of hostile input. inbuf[] is a small fixed-size member (5 bytes in qpdf) and pos is the count of valid digits; the memory safety of this flush() depends entirely on the invariant 1 <= pos <= 5 being upheld by the not-shown append path.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Definition of the flush() member of Pl_ASCII85Decoder. flush() has no parameters and no return value; it operates entirely on member state (this->pos, this->inbuf) and on the downstream pipeline reachable via getNext(). It is the routine that converts one buffered group of ASCII85 digit characters into raw output bytes and emits them. It is invoked both when a full group of 5 digits has been collected and at end-of-stream to drain a partial (2-4 digit) group.

Early-out guard: if pos is 0 there are no buffered digits, so flush is a no-op and returns immediately. The QTC::TC() call is qpdf's test-coverage hook that records that this no-op branch was exercised (it has no runtime security effect). Crucially, this guard establishes that on every path past this point pos >= 1, which is what makes the later subtraction this->pos - 1 non-negative.

The base-85 accumulation. lval is a 32/64-bit unsigned accumulator initialised to 0. The loop always iterates i = 0..4 (all 5 slots of inbuf), regardless of how many digits were actually collected (this->pos). For each digit it does the standard positional base-85 conversion lval = lval*85 + digit, where the digit value is inbuf[i] - 33 ('!' == 33 is base-85 digit 0, 'u' == 117 is digit 84). The 33U literal is unsigned, so the subtraction is unsigned arithmetic. Because the loop reads all 5 slots even for a partial group, the not-shown code path must have padded the unused high-order slots with the maximum digit 'u' (117); the reset on line 30 (memset(inbuf, 117, 5)) is exactly this padding being restored for the next group.

The 32-bit value in lval is decomposed into 4 output bytes. outbuf is a 4-byte stack buffer, zero-initialised. The loop walks i = 3 down to 0, taking the low 8 bits of lval into outbuf[i] and shifting lval right by 8 each iteration, so outbuf ends up big-endian (outbuf[0] is the most significant byte). The loop indices 3..0 are compile-time constants and stay strictly within outbuf[0..3], so this store loop itself is in-bounds. Only the first pos-1 of these 4 bytes are meaningful for a partial group; the rest are the padding artefact.

A second test-coverage hook distinguishing a full group flush (pos == 5, case 0) from a partial group flush (pos < 5, case 1). This is instrumentation only, but it documents the design intent: pos is expected to lie in the range 1..5, with 5 being the full-group case. It has no effect on program data.

The number of output bytes to emit is computed as t = pos - 1 (an ASCII85 group of n digits decodes to n-1 raw bytes: 5 digits -> 4 bytes, 2 digits -> 1 byte). The type of t is deduced by 'auto' from this->pos: if pos is an unsigned type (e.g. size_t, as it is in qpdf), then pos - 1 is an UNSIGNED subtraction. The pos==0 guard on line 4 guarantees pos >= 1 here, so t is a well-defined value in 0..(pos-1). The code then deliberately resets pos to 0 and repads inbuf with 117 ('u') BEFORE calling the downstream write — the comment explains this is exception-safety hygiene so that if getNext()->write() throws, the decoder is left in a clean, empty state rather than re-flushing stale digits.

The decoded bytes are forwarded to the next Pipeline stage: getNext() returns the successor pipeline and write(outbuf, t) hands it t bytes starting at outbuf. Because outbuf is exactly 4 bytes and the length argument is t = pos - 1, this call is memory-safe only if t <= 4, i.e. only if pos <= 5. All of this routine's memory safety therefore reduces to the single invariant 1 <= pos <= 5, which must be enforced by the write()/append path that fills inbuf and increments pos.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This access reads this->inbuf[0..4], a fixed-size (5-byte in qpdf) member array that is filled by the untrusted PDF stream via the not-shown write()/append path. flush()'s safety depends on the append path enforcing that inbuf holds at most 5 bytes and that pos is bounded to 5. If that path ever stores a 6th (or later) digit into inbuf[pos] without checking pos against sizeof(inbuf), it overflows inbuf on the way in — a classic out-of-bounds WRITE (CWE-787), which is precisely the labelled bug class for this sample and the latent defect this fixed flush() presupposes was corrected in the caller. This flush() cannot itself prevent it; it can only be safe if the contract 'inbuf is a 5-element buffer and pos never exceeds 5' holds.

t is derived directly from this->pos and used as the byte length passed to getNext()->write(outbuf, t), but outbuf is only 4 bytes. The pos==0 guard rules out the pos-1 unsigned underflow (pos>=1 so t>=0), so the residual hazard is the UPPER bound: if the append path ever lets pos exceed 5 (e.g. an off-by-one that permits pos==6), then t = pos-1 becomes 5 or more, and write() will read past the end of the 4-byte outbuf (out-of-bounds READ, CWE-125) and forward those extra bytes downstream, potentially overflowing the successor pipeline's destination (propagating to CWE-787). The invariant the implementation must uphold is 1 <= pos <= sizeof(inbuf) == 5, so that t is always in 0..4 and fits within outbuf.

The loop unconditionally consumes all 5 inbuf slots even on a partial flush where pos < 5. This is only correct if slots inbuf[pos..4] have been pre-padded with the digit 'u' (117); the repad on line 30 restores this AFTER a flush, but the FIRST flush of the object relies on the constructor (not shown) initialising inbuf to 117. If that initial padding invariant is ever broken, the loop reads stale/uninitialised bytes as base-85 digits (CWE-457/CWE-125 read of indeterminate data), producing wrong output — not a memory-safety break here since the read stays within the 5-byte array, but a correctness/data-integrity contract the constructor must uphold.

Sign/range hygiene on the digit conversion: inbuf[i] is treated as a base-85 digit via inbuf[i] - 33U. Valid ASCII85 digit characters are '!'(33) .. 'u'(117), giving digit values 0..84. If the append path ever stores a byte < 33 into inbuf, the unsigned subtraction inbuf[i] - 33U underflows to a very large unsigned value (CWE-191), and lval*85 + that value yields a garbage 32-bit result; conversely a byte in 118..255 yields a digit > 84 so lval can exceed the 0..85^5-1 range the encoding guarantees. This is a decode-correctness defect, not a memory-safety one, but it shows flush() trusts the writer to have already rejected non-digit characters; the invariant is 'every byte in inbuf is in [33,117]'.

summary: This is the fixed revision of qpdf's Pl_ASCII85Decoder::flush(). As shown, the loop bounds (i in 0..4 on a 5-byte inbuf, i in 3..0 on a 4-byte outbuf) are in-range, and the pos==0 guard prevents the pos-1 unsigned underflow, so flush() itself contains no directly-exploitable out-of-bounds access. Its memory safety is entirely contingent on the not-shown write()/append path enforcing 1 <= pos <= 5 and inbuf holding only valid base-85 digits; the labelled CWE-787 lives in that append path (an unbounded store into the fixed 5-byte inbuf), and the length t = pos-1 handed to a 4-byte outbuf is safe only while that invariant holds.

reviewed_anchors.reason: No parameters/return; operates on member state only — surface is the members it touches, covered by the pos/inbuf anchors.
Correct guard; it is what prevents the pos-1 unsigned underflow, so it removes rather than creates a risk.
qpdf test-coverage instrumentation; no runtime data effect.
Local accumulator; base-85 of 5 digits (max 85^5-1 = 4437053124) fits in unsigned long; only low 32 bits are used via the shift-out loop.
Reads all 5 slots of the fixed inbuf on partial flush; relies on padding invariant and on the append path bounding inbuf — the central attack surface.
Unsigned subtraction underflows if a byte < 33 was stored; trusts writer to have validated digit range.
Fixed 4-byte destination whose emitted length is the attacker-influenced t = pos-1; safe only if pos <= 5.
In-bounds zeroing of the 4-byte buffer; benign.
Compile-time-constant indices 3..0 stay within outbuf[0..3]; in-bounds.
Length driver for the downstream write; unsigned subtraction (underflow blocked by line-4 guard) but upper bound depends on the pos<=5 invariant.
Deliberate exception-safety reset per the comment; correct and defensive.
Re-establishes the 'u'(117) padding invariant the accumulation loop depends on; ties inbuf size (5) to the loop bound; its correctness underpins partial-flush safety.
Emits t bytes from a 4-byte buffer to the next pipeline stage; OOB read/downstream overflow if t > 4 (i.e. pos > 5).
Fixed-size buffer paired with the pos count field — per method step 3 this pairing must get a finding; the labelled CWE-787 is an unbounded store into it in the append path.
Attacker-influenced count that must satisfy 1 <= pos <= 5; every memory-safety property of flush() reduces to this bound.
Standard qpdf Pipeline successor accessor; the risk is the length argument (covered by the write anchor), not getNext() itself.

findings.title: Fixed 5-byte inbuf driven by pos count — unbounded append is an out-of-bounds write
findings.anchor: this->inbuf[i] fed by this->pos (fixed buffer + count field)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write into fixed-size buffer (buffer + length/count field)
findings.invariant: The write()/append path that fills inbuf must reject digits once pos == sizeof(inbuf) (5): it must never store into inbuf[pos] without checking pos < 5, and must call flush() to drain before accepting a 6th digit. flush() must be able to assume 1 <= pos <= 5.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted ASCII85 stream that supplies a run of base-85 digit characters longer than a group without the append path flushing/bounding pos would store past inbuf[4] (into inbuf[5], inbuf[6], ...), corrupting adjacent object memory — a remote out-of-bounds write triggered purely by parsing a hostile PDF. This flush() is the fixed revision that presumes the caller now enforces the bound; the latent defect is the append-side store.
findings.what_to_confirm: In Pl_ASCII85Decoder::write(), confirm inbuf is declared as a 5-element array and that every store into inbuf indexes inbuf[pos] only while pos < 5, incrementing pos and calling flush() at pos == 5 so pos can never reach 6.

findings.title: write() length t = pos-1 can exceed the 4-byte outbuf if the pos<=5 invariant is violated
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read of source buffer / length exceeds buffer, propagating to downstream overflow
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0225_cwe787.cpp (length safe only under the caller's pos<=5 contract)
findings.invariant: 1 <= this->pos <= 5 so that t = pos-1 is always in 0..4 and never exceeds the 4-byte outbuf passed to write().
findings.failure_mode: If pos ever exceeds 5 (an off-by-one in the append path), t becomes >= 5 and getNext()->write(outbuf, t) reads past outbuf[3] and forwards those out-of-bounds bytes into the next pipeline stage's buffer, which can overflow downstream. The pos==0 guard already blocks the opposite (pos-1 unsigned underflow) failure, so only the upper bound remains as a contract on the caller.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no code path can leave pos > 5 when flush() runs; assert pos <= 5 (or pos <= sizeof(inbuf)) before computing t, or clamp t to sizeof(outbuf).

findings.title: Unsigned base-85 digit conversion underflows for bytes < 33
findings.bug_class: Unsigned integer underflow producing corrupt decode value
findings.invariant: Every byte stored in inbuf must be a valid ASCII85 digit in ['!'(33) .. 'u'(117)]; the write()/append path must reject or terminate on any byte outside that range before it reaches inbuf.
findings.failure_mode: If a byte value < 33 reaches inbuf[i], inbuf[i] - 33U (unsigned) wraps to a huge value; a byte in 118..255 yields a digit > 84. Either way lval is corrupted and the 4 decoded bytes are wrong. This is a decode-correctness/data-integrity issue (the read stays within the 5-byte array), demonstrating flush() trusts upstream validation rather than checking digit range itself.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm write() only admits characters in ['!'..'u'] (plus the 'z' zero-run and '~' terminator handling) into inbuf, so flush() never sees an out-of-range digit byte.

findings.title: Partial-flush loop reads all 5 slots and depends on 'u'(117) padding being pre-initialised
findings.anchor: for (int i = 0; i < 5; ++i) ... this->inbuf[i]; and memset(this->inbuf, 117, 5)
findings.bug_class: Use of possibly-uninitialised buffer contents (in-bounds indeterminate read)
findings.invariant: inbuf[pos..4] must always be padded with 117 ('u', the max digit) before flush() runs on a partial group; the constructor must initialise inbuf to 117 and line 30 must restore it after every flush.
findings.failure_mode: On a partial flush (pos < 5) the loop still reads inbuf[pos..4]. If the object's very first flush occurs before the constructor set inbuf to 117 (or if some path skips the line-30 repad), those slots contain stale/indeterminate bytes that are decoded as digits, corrupting the output. The read stays within the 5-byte array so it is not a memory-safety violation, but it is a correctness contract on object initialisation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the Pl_ASCII85Decoder constructor initialises inbuf to 117 for all 5 bytes and that every non-error exit from flush()/write() leaves inbuf repadded, so a partial flush never reads unpadded slots.

audit_checklist: In Pl_ASCII85Decoder::write(): verify inbuf is exactly 5 bytes and every store indexes inbuf[pos] only while pos < 5, flushing at pos == 5 so pos can never reach 6 (prevents the CWE-787 OOB write into inbuf).
Confirm this->pos is never > 5 at flush() entry; consider an explicit assert(pos <= 5) before t = pos - 1, or clamp t to sizeof(outbuf), so the getNext()->write(outbuf, t) length can never exceed 4.
Verify the append path rejects any byte outside ['!'(33)..'u'(117)] before it enters inbuf, so inbuf[i] - 33U cannot underflow or exceed digit 84.
Confirm the constructor initialises all 5 bytes of inbuf to 117 ('u') and that every flush/reset path repads inbuf, so partial-flush reads of inbuf[pos..4] never see indeterminate data.

Check the exception-safety intent (lines 26-30): confirm pos is reset and inbuf repadded before getNext()->write() on every path, and that a throw from a downstream write() leaves the decoder in a clean empty state with no double-flush of the same group.

Verify the pos type ('auto t' deduction): if pos is unsigned, re-confirm the line-4 pos==0 guard is the only thing preventing t = pos-1 from underflowing to SIZE_MAX, and that no refactor can remove that guard.
